author: @lisapop5219
text: 😂 I check for bedbugs too! I learned to do it the hard way.  My poor husband turned out to be highly allergic to them.  We were moving back to the US from Korea and living in motels.  I had to spend the day washing every item we had,  vacuuming the luggage and then wrapping almost everything in black plastic and putting them on the roof to bake in the August Georgia sun. I have been paranoid ever since.  That was 14 years ago

replies.author: @__jonbud______________________
replies.text: That's what I was wondering. Use the bathroom sink, wring it out real good after mushing/agitating the fabric in soapy water, rinse, re-wring it, stick it in between a folded over towel for a bit, and then hang to dry. 3 minutes of washing or rinsing unless you want to also wash some pants or a shirt. Or just wear your clothes in the shower (after showering yourself first) and soap it up while it's on you and rinse.

replies.text: ​@v.j.3029rice maker is a new one for me. Most hotels in the states ive stayed in have the tiny 1 cup coffee makers. I feel like id be equalky afraid of the rice cooker.

replies.author: @v.j.3029
replies.text: ​@Smittenz1 Well to be clear, it was an apartment in Vietnam that provided the rice maker. I didn't use it anyway. We had a kitchen with a washing machine so that's where I washed my underwear.😉 I never travelled to the US, only to Asia. That could explain why I've never seen a coffee machine in a hotel room.

​@robsomethin4415If it is the last day, debatable. I also am under the impression, if you're going on a trip...you should probably plan on how many clothes you'll need for said trip.
